sentences.text: No.
That's what the barbed wire was for.
You could not talk with them.
They couldn't talk to us.
Being seen near them was even punishable.
They were completely isolated because they, being [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] religiously motivated to help others, were completely separated so that they could not contaminate the other prisoners.
It was-- we marched by every day.
And we saw them marching by.
They would do hard labor.
So they had to get in and out of their barracks.
And some of them who worked outside-- out of the camp.
No.
Nothing.
Just I'd be worked on, so to speak.
It was strictly passive.
Yes, I did something.
But that was-- being one of the youngest and the strongest, I always went for the food.
I had to carry very heavy pockets, and they insisted that we did not rest until we walked or ran.
We could not walk.
We had to run across the parade crowds until we came to the back street.
We could not set them down.
If we set the bucket down, they said it's too heavy for you.
It's a [INAUDIBLE].
I was not in charge, but one of the detail.
That was at the administration building in the kitchen, which was on the other side of the parade route.
Were you with anybody at all that was very well known, or a famous personality, infamous personality?
Yes.
It depends.
Famous for who?
I would like to tell you a little story.
During Christmas time, I was [INAUDIBLE].
It was a day of rest.
And a few inmates-- prisoners, Jews-- put up a little sketch and [INAUDIBLE] others in various [? parks ?] to entertain us.
One of us was a famous bass from the upper of Prague.
His name was [?
Joseph ?] Goodman.
There was another Goodman in Berlin, who was a famous baritone, William Goodman-- Wilhelm Goodman.
And he sang for us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can-- he sang for us [SPEAKING FRENCH], which is a patriotic French song, and which has as its bass melody.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And as he came to the end, which [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], he lowered his voice, so it couldn't be heard and recognized.
He died later, there.
Another one was a famous entertainer from Vienna, who [? at one ?] [? time ?] entertained us.
His name was Leopold [GERMAN].
[GERMAN]
[GERMAN]
And he was very humorous and really made us almost laugh.
He also died later.
The story about those two was-- I know definitely of [?
Joseph ?] Goodman-- he was in the same barrack as I was-- that he had non-Jewish wife, who hated him or wanted to divorce him.
She wanted to get rid of him, whatever it is.
And she wouldn't do anything to help him.
And maybe even she made sure he wouldn't get out.
He was-- he never had-- he had no family, nobody to do anything for him.
And he was extremely lonely and depressed.
And I have a suspicion that the situation with [GERMAN] was similar.
It was a monologue.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goodman, he sang a song without accompaniment.
He sang a few others, too, all without accompaniment.
[GERMAN] recited two or three of his own monologues, for which he was famous.
I don't know the words.
I know the content of it.
It was a text, but that's-- that's actually material.
It cannot be duplicated.
Yes.
They had a very fine medical clinic.
There was only one thing wrong.
If you reported for sick-- sick hall, they called you a faker and punished you.
So it was very dangerous to go to-- to go to sick hall.
And I tell one story.
I don't want to [INAUDIBLE].
It's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother, where we lived in the United States, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went to a party in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] New York.
He was a bachelor, and he met another-- there was a group of fellows.
And if-- somebody called out.
He said, your name is Selling.
He says, yes.
You know Martin Selling?
And he was a little dramatic.
He said, he saved my life.
What actually happened for us, he-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS] he got sick with a cold.
And he was afraid, rightly so, to go to sick hall.
And I-- having a little bit of experience with my mother being sick, I took care of him.
And we actually made it possible for him to stay around and not to sick hall for two or three days until he got better.
And I took care of him at that time, made sure he got something to eat and whatever is necessary.
What's parallel is another fellow got extremely sick.
He also-- also a young kid.
He does not-- he didn't want to go to sick hall.
I took care of him.
I'll just help you a little, too.
But everybody was afraid of infections.
Somehow, I was not.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finally, we had to give him up.
He was diagnosed with meningitis.
And this camp doctor, who was proud of his profession, did everything to pull him through, and he pulled him through, while hundreds around him died from neglect.
I don't know the name.
He was an SS man.
He was in the SS.
He was an SS man.
No.
No.
No, I did not.
I made sure he had blankets, made sure he had water.
And when he was restless, I washed his face and-- all-- what you do with a person with a high temperature.
No.
There was none.
There couldn't have been one.
Till the 27th of January, 1939, which was five weeks.
I was released, as I told you-- released periodically in drips and drops.
And while I was in Dachau, my aunt got-- and other relatives, got permission from a refugee camp in England that I can go there till my registration number for the United States came up.
And I was told I have to wait several years before I finally could get in under the quota system.
You're familiar with the quota system, I presume?
Yeah.
So--
I don't want to talk about it.
It's generally known.
And there are better sources in that respect.
I have to keep it personal.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was released under the condition that I leave Germany within six months.
And the only place I could go was a refugee camp.
And that was a condition that--
Under which I was released.
He was released three days earlier.
He had a chance to come to the United States.
And we met up again in the United States.
Oh, sure.
Actually, I was the last of those eight.
Yeah.
To Munich and Lehrberg.
In fact, we had to leave Lehrberg because the house was confiscated and everything.
And I went to my aunt, where my brother lived.
Yes.
I don't have it here.
We were allowed to write one letter a week--
--which was censored.
We couldn't write much, because it was strictly censored.
And we were-- we were told what not to write.
Almost anything.
All we could say-- we are fine.
We could not complain.
We could not-- if any-- we could not mention any sickness or any-- any other handicap, or whatever it is.
We had to be fine, or we couldn't write.
And if we wrote we weren't fine, they made sure we were not fine.
Yes.
Once a week.
From my brother and my aunt.
In Lehrberg.
Similar.
Of course.
Everything was censored.
There was nothing which wasn't censored.
No.
I-- as I said, they were meaningless, except we are here.
We were put on a train and shipped to Munich and said we were on our own.
I stayed there only overnight, until I went to Nuremberg.
Yeah.
Six months later-- I-- four months-- five months later, I went to stay at a refugee camp in England.
Yeah.
Nothing.
I kept busy trying to get my things ready and to get passport and things like that.
And--
No.
That came later.
Yeah.
After I had left.
That was the-- after the war started was they had special identification cards.
On the 30th of July.
Or 30th of June, 1939.
It's in my passport.
By myself.
No.
He had not-- he didn't have permission to come to the refugee camp, because not being in Dachau, he was not considered endangered.
Yeah.
To the refugee camp in England.
By train and across the Channel.
It took about almost a day.
It wasn't my own.
It was another transport.
Actually, we were collected in Cologne and went there as a group, just for transportation purposes.
Yeah.
We were-- on that transport, we were about 30.
From all over Germany.
I didn't know anyone.
They were men, but some were married and their wives were on the same train, but not with us.
Yeah.
I don't know it was unusual.
It was, of course-- it had the J, and being Jewish and the visa to go to England.
You had to have visas at that time.
And my particular visa was restricted to that refugee camp.
If I wanted to get out of it-- once visiting-- I went Edinburgh to visit a cousin who also was a refugee there.
I had to get permission from the local police.
Yeah.
I don't know whether it was official or unofficial.
It was called the Kitchener Camp.
I think officially it was a refugee-- Jewish refugee camp.
That was in the county of Kent near Richborough, not too far from Ramsgate.
It was an old British army barrack.
That's where the name Kitchener Camp came from.
It was used for the British Expeditionary Force in World War One.
And it fell too-- and there was very little left of it, so it was bought by a Jewish relief organization-- mostly American money-- and restored, mostly with the help of the [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know.
About a thousand, I would say.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think it was able to hold more.
Primitive.
More primitive than Dachau.
Toilet facilities, rooms-- rooms, of course.
We had no heat in the barracks.
There were army cots.
It was a refugee-- run by a refugee company.
I don't even know.
I-- at that time, I didn't care much about anything anymore.
I just drifted along wherever I was pushed.
I was not much interested in my surroundings.
Nothing.
Yes, everybody of course had to help with the maintenance, chores, and so on.
And being naturally inclined, I wound up taking-- in the hospital.
We had a little hospital there-- being a helper.
Yeah.
Mostly colds, maybe a few injuries.
Serious ones were shipped to the local hospital in Ramsgate.
Yeah.
There were two Englishmen, and the rest were, I think, [INAUDIBLE] from Germany.
Refugees.
Refugees.
Yeah.
No.
We were allowed to leave within five miles of the camp.
So I also had a bike I bought.
I could go to Ramsgate.
It was summer.
I could go to the beach or go to Richborough, Sandwich, which was a close by town.
That was permissible.
And that five-mile limit was flexible.
Yes.
We were there-- I was there less than two months when the war in Europe broke out.
And pretty soon afterwards, the British liked to-- would like to get the camp back.
They [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started out first recruiting some of us for the Pioneer Corps, which was a fancy name for a labor battalion.
And they were actually able to form out of-- from the refugees to [INAUDIBLE], which in the beginning were part of the camp in one section.
But later on, just after I left, they deported all non-military and interred some on the Isle of Man.
So they went from one internment camp to another.
And the British considered us Germans and treated us accordingly.
They hated Jews to start out with, and they were not very fond of refugees.
I could tell stories, mostly hearsay, some personal, about how the British react to the German refugees in England.
It's not very salutary.
No, I don't.
Actually, nothing happened to me, personally, but-- because I left.
But quite a few others, they were interred in the Isle of Man and stayed there for two years, and so on.
In that-- in the internment camp in that Isle of Man was not only people from the camp, but refugees, domestics mostly, who worked as domestics in England.
That was the only type of job which was permitted.
Between England and Ireland.
I had very little contact, so I cannot complain much.
Half a year.
No.
We were-- it was a holding camp, waiting to get to [INAUDIBLE] to get out of it one way or another.
It was just a temporary holding to get out of Germany until we were able to get someplace else.
It happened [INAUDIBLE] and [?
Steve ?] [INAUDIBLE] a friend of mine was also there.
And he also came to the United States, and we've still stayed friends.
And I knew a few local people.
But otherwise they were just shadows.
Not at all.
I got my permit to enter the United States.
My quota number came up, and I went to-- had to go to the consulate in London.
I got my passport and visa, and I came to the United States.
That was-- I left England on the 30th of January, 1939, and I arrived on the 12th of February.
On a British ship.
Yes, it was the Georgic.
Georgic-- G-E-O-R-G-I-G-- C.
G-I-G.
It was a Cunard liner.
One of the reasons I couldn't get there after I got out of Dachau, and I had enough money on me directly, from cash.
So I could walk into a Cunard line office and building and bought a first class ticket on the Queen Mary to the United States, open-- an open ticket.
With the help of that ticket, I got a ticket on the Cunard line.
Most people at the Kitchener Camp had tickets-- open tickets to the United States.

[DRINKS] But after the war broke out, the German lines, the French lines, the Italian lines, and all those which normally came from-- stopped in England and went to the United States, wouldn't stop there anymore.
So all the tickets-- Cunard that was the only line which recognized its tickets.
So I had some money to come to the States.
And many people were in camp with me.
And when their quota number came up, they had no money to pay for the passage.
I'm not sure I understand.
You said you came on another ship.
No.
[INAUDIBLE] But they-- yes, but they recognized their own line tickets, the tickets from their own line.
But you couldn't come on the--
I couldn't-- The Queen Mary, [INAUDIBLE].
So the Queen Mary is a troop ship now.
So you came-- you were able to come on another ship.
But I came on another ship on the Cunard line.
I landed in New York.
Of course.
[INAUDIBLE]
I was too numb to think anything.
It was a period for me where I was just drifting along.
I was not enthusiastic about anything.
I just shuffled along.
21 At that time.
My uncle and my brother had come before.
They were able to get directly out of Germany, get their quota number in October of '39.
That was a month and a half after the war broke out in Europe, where many people were trapped in the other parts [INAUDIBLE].
So there were [INAUDIBLE] faster.
And Holland still was a neutral country.
And it was possible to take passage from Amsterdam on the Holland American line and even to pay with German money.
So they came in October 1939. to the United States, directly, while I was still sitting in England waiting to be processed by the consulate.
We lived in Newark.
My-- my uncle and my brother lived in Newark.
